content.text:
Treat tests and @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as source of truth. Carefully review the handling of 'S', 's' branch in `State.add` in @src/liblex.zig 

My approach is explicit handling, only necessary abstraction, reduce computing operations.

Consult the Oracle for another perspective. Let me know your finding.

content.thinking: I'll start by reading the relevant files to understand what I'm working with.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
10: - Literal input remains until the new word.
11: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
12: - This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
13: 
14: ### Diacritics
15: 
16: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
17: Tones have a separate section.
18: 
19: #### Empty
20: 
21: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
22: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
23:   characters.
24: 
25: #### Circumflex
26: 
27: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
28: 
29: #### Breve
30: 
31: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
32: 
33: #### Horn
34: 
35: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
36: 
37: #### Stroke
38: 
39: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
40: 
41: #### Apply rules
42: 
43: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
44: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
45:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
46:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
47:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
48: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
49:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
50:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
51:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
52: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
53: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
54: 
55: #### Override rules
56: 
57: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
58:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
59:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
60: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
61: - Keep tone untouched.
62: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
63: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
64: 
65: #### Cancel rules
66: 
67: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
68:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
69: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
70:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
71:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
72:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
73:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
74: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
75: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
76:   remains until the new word.
77:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
78: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
79: 
80: #### Auto-fill rules
81: 
82: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
83:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
84: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
85:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
86:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
87:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
88: - The new input character is appended literally.
89: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
90: 
91: ### Tones
92: 
93: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
94: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
95: These vowel could take any tone.
96: 
97: #### Level (ngang)
98: 
99: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
100:   corresponding variants.
101: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
102: 
103: #### Rising (sắc)
104: 
105: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
106: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
107:   -> `Ớ`.
108: 
109: #### Falling (huyền)
110: 
111: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
112: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
113:   -> `Ờ`.
114: 
115: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
116: 
117: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
118: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
119:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
120: 
121: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
122: 
123: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
124: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
125:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
126: 
127: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
128: 
129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
211:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I need to scan through the rest of the file to locate the 'S' and 's' branch.
I need to continue reviewing the test file to check the tests for uppercase and lowercase 'S', along with the apply_tone, reset_tone functions and the rest of the implementation.
I want to check the rest of the file to see what else is there.

Looking at the 'S' and 's' case branch in the code, I'm examining how it handles rising tones and checking for issues around literal input mode and buffer management.

Continuing through the logic, I'm checking whether the pseudoword has vowels, and if not, appending the character literally and clearing the modification index. Then I'm looking at the special case of 'QU' where 'U' acts as a consonant rather than a vowel, which also gets appended literally. Now I'm moving into the tone position detection logic by iterating through the vowel range to find where the tone mark should be placed.

Once I've identified the tone position, I handle three main scenarios: if there's no existing tone, I apply a rising tone directly; if there's a different tone already present, I reset it and apply the rising tone instead; or if the rising tone is already there, I cancel it by resetting and then switch to literal input mode. I'm noticing a difference in how the 'S' branch handles literal cases compared to the 'F' branch—specifically around when the literal_index gets set.

Looking at the spec more carefully, I see that F, J, and W are the only characters that trigger a permanent switch to literal mode, while S, R, and X are valid Vietnamese letters that shouldn't trigger that switch. So when 'S' gets appended literally due to missing vowels, we shouldn't activate literal mode permanently like we would with F. The question is whether the cancel rules about literal appends apply differently here, and I need to trace through what happens when a user types something like "bs"—the 'b' and 's' both get appended as consonants, but then if a vowel appears later, how should the system respond? Looking at the cancel branch, it correctly resets only the matched tone (since there's at most one tone in the assertion), then sets literal_index and appends literally. The buffer_modification_index is properly tracked through reset_tone and apply_tone in both cancel and override cases. I'm now checking what happens when literal_index is already set from a previous operation.

For the QU special case, I'm noticing the tone check seems redundant — it explicitly checks that U has level tone after already calling equals_ignore_case_and_tone, which ignores tone anyway. The real question is whether U could have picked up a tone from earlier in the same word, but given how pseudoword scanning works on the trailing syllable, if QU exists with a tone on U, that would trigger cancel/override first before reaching this check.

Looking at the inconsistency between branches: the 'S' branch is stricter about QU validation (checking both case-insensitive equality and tone level), while the 'F' branch just checks the base characters without diacritic validation. The spec says QU is consonant-only and can't take tone, so the 'S' branch's approach is more correct — if U has a horn diacritic, it's no longer a pure QU pair. The 'F' branch is incomplete anyway, so that's less critical.

There's also some redundancy in how the code chains multiple `else if` conditions that all check `tone_index != null`, which could be simplified structurally. And I'm spotting what looks like a potential issue in the cancel branch — it might not be resetting `buffer_modification_index` before calling `reset_tone`.

Actually, looking closer at the add function, it does set `buffer_modification_index = null` at line 281 before the switch, so that's fine. The `apply_tone` function has an assertion that all vowels are level, which holds because `reset_tone` is called first in the override branch. The tracking of earliest modification position looks correct too — `reset_tone` sets an initial index, then `apply_tone` only updates if it finds an earlier position.

Let me trace through a concrete example with `tìê` and input 's' to verify the logic.

Checking buffer overflow... Checking the literal_index positioning when appending characters and verifying the state transitions are correct — specifically that when we append 's' literally because there are no vowels, literal_index isn't set, which allows future processing to handle it properly. Now I'm walking through a concrete example where the buffer contains 'bao' and the user types 's' to trace how the tone application logic handles multiple vowels and checks for exact patterns like OA/OE/OO/UY. Now I'm identifying the key issues in the implementation. There's redundant null checking that could be simplified, and the vowel scanning could short-circuit on the first match instead of iterating through all of them. The QU detection logic differs between the 'F' and 'S' branches — the 'S' version is stricter and more correct according to the spec, checking for tone markers on the U, so the 'F' branch should align with that approach. There's also an unreachable code path in the tone assignment chain that could be cleaned up.

The direct reset approach would be more efficient than calling the generalized `reset_tone` helper since we already know the tone index, though there's a trade-off with code reusability. The cancel branch's `literal_index` assignment happens before appending the new character, which is actually correct — it marks the position of the newly added literal — but the logic could be clearer. One more thing to check: the cancel branch also calls `reset_tone`, which means it's scanning all vowels unnecessarily when we could directly reset the known tone position.

Looking at the `apply_tone` function, it appears to always succeed for words with vowels since the final fallback rule covers all cases by placing tone on the first vowel, so the spec's mention of potential failure doesn't actually occur in practice. The `buffer_modification_index` tracking after cancel is correct too — it reflects the earliest modified position from the reset, which is what we want since the newly appended character sits at the end. There's also an interesting asymmetry: when 'W' is appended literally, `literal_index` gets set if needed, but 'S' at the start doesn't set it because S can be a valid Vietnamese starting consonant.

Now I'm tracing through some test cases to verify the logic. For a word like `sas`, the first 's' appends literally since the buffer is empty, then 'a' also appends literally since the previous character isn't a vowel, and finally the second 's' triggers tone application on the 'a'. For `qus`, I'm checking how the QU handling works when we have those characters followed by 's'. word.length=3 so the QU exception doesn't apply here, and the tone gets placed at vowels_start + 1 instead. I'm also noticing some optimization opportunities: the vowel-scanning loop could break early once it finds the tone, reset_tone could accept the tone_index directly instead of rescanning, and there's a dead unreachable branch I can remove. For the QU exception itself, I should verify the interaction with cancel/override when U already has a tone mark applied. The distinction between a true QU consonant pair with no tone versus a U that happens to have an existing tone is important for correctness. I'm also checking for potential bugs in the override flow — specifically whether `apply_tone` might place the tone at a different position after `reset_tone` clears all vowels, and whether `apply_tone` could fail to place a tone, leaving an inconsistent state. Based on the spec, `apply_tone` should always succeed for non-QU pseudo-words with vowels, so this shouldn't be an issue. I'm also noticing some redundant predicates like repeated `tone_index != null` checks that are stylistically worth flagging, and there's a dead `else { unreachable }` branch to consider.

Looking at the broader code quality, I see several areas worth documenting: the for loop over vowels could break early after finding the first match, `reset_tone` over-scans when we already know the tone index and could reset in-place, there's an incomplete doc comment around line 94-95, and there's an inconsistency in how the 'F' and 'S' branches handle the QU check — 'S' is more semantically correct by checking for diacritics and tone, while 'F' uses a simpler approach. The cancel branch's logic of setting `literal_index` before `append_literal` is subtle and could use a clarifying comment. I'm also noticing there's no test coverage for the 'S' branch's cancel rule, QU exception, or no-vowels case.

Let me verify the `literal_index` positioning: if buffer_length is 1 before the append, setting `literal_index = 1` means it points to where the new character will land after `append_literal` increments the buffer. That checks out. Inside `append_literal` there's a conditional that won't re-set `literal_index` since it's already assigned, which is fine. For the `buffer_modification_index` — it tracks the earliest modified position for synthetic backspace calculation, so pointing it to the reset position while appending a new character at the end is semantically correct since the caller needs to know the full range of changes.

content.text: Let me check the apply_tone function and consult Oracle for another view.

input.task:
Review the 'S', 's' branch in State.add (lines 681-745) in src/liblex.zig against the Vietnamese Telex spec. Specifically focus on:

1. Correctness vs the spec (sections "Apply rules", "Cancel rules", "Override rules" under Tones).
2. Redundant or dead code.
3. Unnecessary computations / scans (the user values "reduce computing operations").
4. Edge cases not covered by tests.
5. Consistency vs the 'F', 'f' branch (which is incomplete).
6. Any subtle bugs around buffer_modification_index, literal_index, or tone_index handling.

Constraints from the user:
- "Explicit handling, only necessary abstraction, reduce computing operations."
- Tests and the spec are the source of truth.

Please be specific: cite line numbers, quote spec phrases, and flag any actual defects vs purely stylistic suggestions.
input.context: The 'S','s' branch handles the rising tone (sắc) trigger. It guards literal/empty-buffer cases, scans pseudoword, short-circuits on no-vowels and QU exception, then finds existing tone and routes to apply / override / cancel paths. The reset_tone helper is at line ~1137 and apply_tone at ~1017. Tests for S start at line 2316. Spec is at specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md.
